analogy: Think of "Sound" through the chapter's own opening images: Have you ever wondered why some plants can survive long droughts while others cannot? Or, how certain insects protect crops while others damage them? The Earth is home to an enormous variety of life forms. From microscopic organisms invisible to the naked eye to giant... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: 229 Patterns in Life: Diversity and Classification, 12.1 India as a Biodiversity Hotspot, 12.2 How has the Biodiversity Evolved?, 12.3 How to Classify Organisms?. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Sound is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that have you ever wondered why some plants can survive long droughts while others cannot?

The text explains that or, how certain insects protect crops while others damage them?

The text explains that the Earth is home to an enormous variety of life forms.

The text explains that from microscopic organisms invisible to the naked eye to giant trees, from glowing jellyfish to soaring eagles, life exists in countless forms and habitats — from the snow-clad Himalayas to the coral reefs of the Andaman Sea.

The text explains that this immense variety of living organisms is known as biodiversity.

The text explains that biodiversity is essential for life on the Earth.

The text explains that every organism plays a role in keeping nature stable and functioning.

The text explains that microscopic algae in the oceans release most of the oxygen we breathe.

The text explains that fungi and bacteria decompose fallen leaves, and convert waste into manure, making the soil fertile.

The text explains that birds, bees and bats pollinate flowers, while plants capture sunlight to prepare food that support nearly all life on the planet.

The text explains that these interconnections help sustain ecosystems and make the Earth suitable for living organisms.

The text explains that humans, too, depend on biodiversity for food, shelter, medicines and livelihoods.

The text explains that for centuries, farmers relied on their practical knowledge to conserve diverse crop varieties with useful Patterns in Life: Diversity and Classification y What do you understand by biodiversity? y How does the grouping of organisms help us understand diversity? y On what basis, are plants and animals classified? y How does classification help address problems in farming?

The text explains that 229 Patterns in Life: Diversity and Classification characteristics, such as drought tolerance, pest resistance and their ability to grow in nutrient poor soils.

The text explains that they realised that diversity reduces the risk of crop failure and strengthens food security.

The text explains that in such a vast diversity, organisms share certain similarities while also showing many differences.

The text explains that to study diversity systematically, scientists group and classify organisms based on their shared characteristics and evolutionary relationships.

The text explains that classification helps us understand how organisms are related, how they function and how we can use this knowledge in activities, such as ecosystem management, biodiversity conservation, sustainable farming, and so on.

The text explains that each of these regions has distinct soil types and different climatic conditions like temperature and rainfall.

The text explains that protecting these regions is equally important, as they support food webs and help ecosystems remain healthy.

The text explains that the biodiversity that we see today on the Earth was not always the same.

The text explains that the study of this biological diversity is possible through a systematic framework that is provided by classification.

The text explains that 12.2 shows the diversity of animals in an ecosystem.

The text explains that ponder on the questions given below: y Which animals can you identify in the given picture? y Where are they seen? y Which animals in the picture seem active: (i) during the day?

The text explains that (day/night/ unsure) Any visible feature(s) Owl Tree Night Feathers 3.

The text explains that this leads to an important question: how do scientists decide which features to use while grouping the living organisms?

The text explains that this question leads us to the idea of classification, a systematic way of organising the Earth’s living diversity.

The text explains that 12.3.1 Some criteria to classify living organisms Scientists often look at broad and easily visible features, and then look at more detailed features.

The text explains that external features — visible characteristics, such as shape, size and body organisation.

The text explains that internal structures — skeletal patterns, presence or absence of organs and different types of tissues.

The text explains that cell structure — whether the organism is unicellular or multicellular, the cell is eukaryote or prokaryote, presence or absence of cell wall.

The text explains that genetic similarity — similarities in inherited features, which scientists study in detail using DNA.

The text explains that these features help in understanding the similarities and differences among organisms.

The text explains that similar features in organisms suggest that they have evolved from common ancestors.

The text explains that if many organisms share common features, could they also share a common ancestry?

The text explains that in the same way, the Earth is home to millions of organisms.

The text explains that therefore, classifying these organisms systematically helps us in understanding them better.

The text explains that studying such patterns allows scientists to ask precise questions about biodiversity, such as — y How are species distributed within a forest?

The text explains that think and discuss the case based on the following questions — (i) How can scientists keep track of so many species?

The text explains that what features can help scientists distinguish them from one another?

The text explains that many organisms show similarities in their external features, internal structure (such as tissues and organs) and cellular organisation.

The text explains that this grouping helps to organise information, understand relationships among organisms and study life in a systematic manner.

The text explains that this scientific system of grouping living organisms is known as biological classification.

The text explains that it makes the study of living organisms more organised and systematic.

The text explains that it allows scientists all over the world to discuss about organisms using a common system.

The text explains that 12.5 Biological Classification Systems Over Time In the previous section, you have learnt to identify various criteria for classifying the huge biodiversity in the world.

The text explains that scientists have developed different classification systems over time, based on the knowledge available.

The text explains that however, this system had limitations because it relied mainly on easily observable external characteristics.

The text explains that in the 18th century, scientists introduced the two kingdom system.

The text explains that 251 Patterns in Life: Diversity and Classification The Journey Beyond y Visit a nearby park or a water body.

The text explains that identify ten organisms and classify them into kingdoms. y Interview a gardener or a forest worker.

The text explains that compare the traditional scientific methods of identification. y If you were studying the Pakke Tiger Reserve or any other bird sanctuary of your choice, how would you organise the information about the bird species? y Conduct a survey in a forest area near a village, identify edible and poisonous mushrooms with the help of indigenous knowledge of community resource person(s). y Collect and study the postal stamps of India and the world released over time on diversified flora and fauna.

Source: NCERT Class 9 Science, Chapter 12: Sound

Do not add outside examples to Sound; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
